Jae Woong Hwang Resume Experience Toolchains

Phase 2: Productization

Sensorless Shift Control & V-Cycle Validation for Heavy-Duty TCU

At ADD, I worked on TCU application software for the 55-ton K2 tracked vehicle transmission. The engineering value was not simply writing control code; it was productizing sensorless shift-control logic under harsh physical constraints and validating it through MILS, HILS, powerpack dynamometer, and actual vehicle testing.

Back to resume
Productization

From Physics to Validated TCU Software

This project sits at the point where controls, embedded software, and real hardware must meet. The core challenge was achieving robust shift quality without direct clutch-pressure sensing while keeping the software viable for real-time validation and deployment.

Problem

Physical Constraints & Sensorless Environment

  • Massive inertia: controlling shifts in a 55-ton tracked vehicle means extreme driveline torque and high consequence for poor shift quality.
  • Hardware limitation: due to cost and durability constraints, individual clutch pressure sensors could not be installed.
  • Sensorless implication: open-loop-only methods were not enough to guarantee repeatable shift quality and reliability.
  • Engineering target: estimate clutch state indirectly from speed and CAN-based signals, then validate the software through HILS, dynamometer, and field testing.
Architecture & Math

HILS Architecture & Real-Time Integration

Real-Time HILS Integration

Bridged theoretical hydro-mechanical dynamics and software validation by integrating nonlinear transmission plant models into the real-time HILS environment before expensive dynamometer and field tests.

Plant Dynamics Utilized

Applied Newton-iteration-based lumped hydraulic models to compute solenoid-valve and clutch-piston pressure behavior while staying within real-time constraints.

ṗ = (βKAαrefF(pA − p + pcent(ω)) − …) / (V0 + xAp)

Clutch Kiss-Point EOL & Adaptation Logic

Designed ASW logic to estimate the clutch kiss-point—the onset of torque transfer—and adapt it over time, enabling an intelligent transition from open-loop filling to closed-loop pressure control.

Core Technical Evidence

Performance Result & Research Output

  • Demonstrated stronger performance than the legacy ZF TCU baseline across shift shock metrics, acceleration behavior, braking behavior, and vehicle response.
  • Added functions beyond the legacy ZF TCU including clutch adaptation, improved EOL calibration logic, towing-start support, and fault-diagnosis information transmission.
  • Validated the application software through the full V-cycle: MILS, HILS, powerpack dynamometer, and actual vehicle field testing.

Academic Papers

  1. Hyunseok Choi, Jaewoong Hwang, Seibum Choi, “Dynamic driveline torque estimation during whole gear shift for an automatic transmission,” Mechanism and Machine Theory, 2018. Published.
  2. Hyunseok Choi, Jaewoong Hwang, Seibum Choi, “Gear shift control based on estimated driveline torque for an automatic transmission,” Mechanism and Machine Theory, 2020. Listed in the source portfolio as forthcoming/publication-stage work.

Research contribution: proposed driveline torque estimation and torque-feedback control based on dynamic analysis of the transmission mechanical model, then verified the method in a dynamometer environment.

Korean Conference Paper

  1. Jaewoong Hwang, Hyunseok Choi, Yongwon Kim, “Design study of a HIL system plant model for integrated TCU software development for tracked vehicles,” Korean Society for Military Science and Technology Annual Conference, 2019.

Korean Patent / Patent Applications

  1. Registered Korean Patent: “Method for estimating automatic-transmission driveline torque and friction coefficient,” Registration No. 10-2155303, registered on 2020.09.07.
  2. Korean Patent Application: “Method for transmitting fault-diagnosis information of an automatic transmission for high-mobility tracked vehicles,” Application No. 10-2020-0015038, filed on 2020.02.07.
  3. Korean Patent Application: “Method for guiding EOL calibration of an automatic transmission for combat vehicles,” Application No. 10-2020-005020, filed on 2020.01.14.
  4. Korean Patent Application: “Method for guiding towed-vehicle start-up device and towed-vehicle start-up operation,” Application No. 10-2019-0110314, filed on 2019.09.05.

Why This Matters

  • Sensorless control: solved a real product constraint rather than assuming ideal instrumentation.
  • Physics-to-software translation: turned hydro-mechanical behavior into executable and testable TCU software logic.
  • Full validation path: connected MILS, HILS, powerpack dynamometer, and actual vehicle verification into a single V-cycle story.
  • Career signal: demonstrates productization ability—moving from abstract control concepts to validated embedded software under real constraints.
